attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); user asks to add, modify, debug, optimize, or improve a Claude feature (prompt caching, cache hit rate, adaptive thinking, compaction, code_execution, batch, files API, citations, memory tool) or a Claude model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; or user asks about prompt caching / cache hit rate / cache reads / cache creation in any project that uses the Anthropic SDK (even without mentioning Claude by name).
DO NOT TRIGGER when: file imports `openai`/non-Anthropic SDK, filename signals another provider (`agent-openai.py`, `*-generic.py`), code is provider-neutral, or task is general programming/ML.

1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

input.prompt:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Policy management system with quote and claim operations

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Requirements (from the kata specification):

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for a list of items, and **claim** processes a damage report against an existing policy.

### Item values and base premiums
- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
- Components (runes, moonstones): 250 G each, 25 G base premium per component
- Building block of 3 alike components: special base premium of 60 G

### Premium modifiers
- Cursed items: +50% risk surcharge (on affected item only)
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment ≥ 5): +30% risk surcharge (on affected item only)
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years): -20% loyalty discount (policy-wide)
- First insurance: +10% initial assessment surcharge (policy-wide)
- Follow-up contracts (after first quote): -15% discount (policy-wide)
- 5 G processing fee added at the very end
- Rounding: in MHPCO's favor (premiums rounded up, payouts rounded down)

### Modifier application order
- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the base premium of the affected item
- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums)
- Processing fee is added at the very end

### Claim processing
- 100 G deductible per damage event (per damaged item)
- Total payout capped at 2× insurance sum
- Enchantment ≥ 8: reimbursed at 50% of damage amount
- Dragon material: fully reimbursed
- Both clauses apply → 50% wins, then deductible
- Dragon material + enchantment < 8 → full reimbursement, then deductible

### Examples from spec:
- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks)
- empty item list → premium 5 G (only fee)
- unknown item type → non-zero exit + error to stderr
- Newcomer cursed sword: 100+50+10+5 = 165 G
- Long-standing 2nd contract cursed enchanted sword: 100+50+30-20+10-15+5 = 160 G
- Regular steel sword damage 500 G → payout 400 G
- Dragon-material sword ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G
- Dragon-material sword ench 5, damage 800 → payout 700 G
- Deductible per item: sword 500 + amulet 300 → payout 600

### Edge cases:
- Claim with damage for item not in policy → error
- Claim with negative damage amount → error
- More damage entries than insured items of that type → error
- Cap exhaustion across multiple claims

### CLI:
- Entry point at src/cli.ts
- Reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout
- Customer object + steps array (quote/claim)
- Results array matching steps

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

```typescript
export function quote(customer: Customer, items: unknown[]): number {
```
```typescript
  if (items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;
  return 110 + PROCESSING_FEE;
}
```

- Bindings: 2 (customer, items) = 2
- Invocations: 3 (property access `items.length`, equality comparison `=== 0`, addition `110 + PROCESSING_FEE`) = 6
- Conditionals: 1 (the `if` statement) = 4
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0

The mass increased slightly because the addition operation `110 + PROCESSING_FEE` introduces an extra invocation compared to the single constant `115`. - Mass: Increased from 12 to 14 (delta +2). The small increase is justified because clarity improved -- the structure of the computation is now visible.
- Rule 2 trumps APP in this case.

content.text:
**Verification of minimality**: The implementation only handles exactly what the three passing tests demand:
- Empty list returns processing fee (5)
- Single sword returns 100 + 10 + 5 = 115
- Single amulet returns 60 + 6 + 5 = 71

I did not add base premiums for staff or potion (those are future tests). I did not generalize to handle multiple items. The `basePremium / 10` computes the 10% first-insurance surcharge using integer-safe division instead of `* 1.1` which caused floating point issues.

---

Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added an `Item` type, a `BASE_PREMIUMS` lookup map (sword: 100, amulet: 60), and changed the quote function to look up the base premium by item type, compute the 10% first-insurance surcharge (`basePremium / 10`), and add the processing fee.

content.text:
**Refactored Code Mass**:
```
export function quote(customer: Customer, items: Item[]): number {
  if (items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;
  const basePremium = BASE_PREMIUMS[items[0].type];
  const firstInsuranceSurcharge = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
  return basePremium + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE;
}
```

Component Count:
- Constants: 1 (literal 0) = 1
- Bindings: 4 (customer, items, basePremium, firstInsuranceSurcharge) = 4
- Invocations: 6 (items.length, === comparison, BASE_PREMIUMS[] lookup, items[0].type access, * multiplication, + addition x2... actually let me recount: `items.length` property access = 1, `===` comparison = 1, `BASE_PREMIUMS[items[0].type]` lookup = 1, `items[0]` access = 1, `.type` access = 1, `basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` = 1, `basePremium + firstInsuranceSurcharge` = 1, `... + PROCESSING_FEE` = 1) = 8 invocations x 2 = 16
- Conditionals: 1 (if statement) = 4
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 2 (const basePremium, const firstInsuranceSurcharge) = 12

Total Mass: 1 + 4 + 16 + 4 + 0 + 12 = **37**

Mass Change: 29 --> 37 (delta +8)

The mass increased, which is expected. The refactoring traded lower mass for better intent revelation (Rule 2 trumps APP). The magic expression `basePremium / 10` is now replaced with a named constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` and an explaining variable `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, making the domain concept of the first-insurance surcharge explicit and self-documenting.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All function and type names already clearly describe their purpose -- no renames needed.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` as a named constant and introduced `firstInsuranceSurcharge` as an explaining variable. The implicit calculation `basePremium / 10` is now self-documenting.
- Mass: Increased from 29 to 37 (delta +8). This is acceptable because Rule 2 (clarity) takes priority over lower mass per the Simple Design Rules priority order.

Benefits:
- The domain concept "first insurance surcharge" is now explicitly named in code
- The rate (10%) is a named constant rather than a magic number, making it easy to find and modify
- The return expression `basePremium + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` reads as a clear sum of three named components

## Step 7: Report Completion

**Mass Change**: 29 --> 37 (acceptable increase; clarity over mass per Rule 2 priority).

The `Math.ceil` is required because 2 runes (50) + 1 moonstone (25) = 75, plus 10% surcharge (7.5) + 5 fee = 87.5, but the test expects 88. I verified that `Math.ceil` does not break any existing test since all prior results are already whole numbers.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

content.text:
## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Before refactoring** (return line):
```typescript
return Math.ceil(basePremium + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE + PROCESSING_FEE);
```
- Invocations: Math.ceil, +, *, + = 4 invocations x 2 = 8
- Bindings referenced: basePremium (x2), FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE, PROCESSING_FEE = 4 x 1 = 4
- Total for this section: 12

**After refactoring** (return + explaining variable):
```typescript
const firstInsuranceSurcharge = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
return Math.ceil(basePremium + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE);
```
- Assignments: 1 (const firstInsuranceSurcharge = ...) = 1 x 6 = 6
- Bindings: 1 new (firstInsuranceSurcharge) = 1
- Invocations: *, Math.ceil, +, + = 4 invocations x 2 = 8
- Bindings referenced: basePremium (x2), FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE, firstInsuranceSurcharge, PROCESSING_FEE = 5 x 1 = 5
- Total for this section: 20

Mass change for the affected section: 12 --> 20 (increase of 8).

The mass increased, but this is a case where **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass**. The explaining variable `firstInsuranceSurcharge` makes it clear what each component of the final calculation represents: base premium + the surcharge amount + processing fee. This is more readable than embedding `basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` inline.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: `quote` already clearly describes purpose -- kept as-is
- Naming: `componentPremium`, constants all clearly named -- kept as-is
- Rule 2 improvement: Extracted explaining variable `firstInsuranceSurcharge` to make the return statement clearer
- First attempt (algebraic simplification with `1 + rate`) failed due to floating-point precision issues with `Math.ceil` -- reverted
- Mass: Increased from ~12 to ~20 for the affected section (Rule 2 trumps APP)
- No duplication found in production code
- Test code has repetitive setup but this is acceptable for test readability

Benefits:
- The return statement now clearly shows three distinct components: base premium, surcharge, and processing fee
- Each component is named, making the formula self-documenting
- Future modifiers will be easier to add alongside this pattern

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted explaining variable `firstInsuranceSurcharge` to improve intent clarity in the return calculation (Rule 2)
- **Mass Change**: Slight increase (~12 to ~20 for affected section), justified by improved readability
- **Tests**: All 10 passing

input.prompt:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "should add 50% cursed surcharge to the cursed item base premium only"
Expected behavior: quote({yearsAsCustomer: 0, quoteCount: 0}, [{type: "sword", material: "steel", enchantment: 0, cursed: true}]) returns 165
Current error: Expected 165, Received 115
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Current implementation computes basePremium from items but doesn't handle cursed surcharge. Need to add a 50% surcharge on the base premium of each cursed item.

The structure per the spec:
- basePremium = sum of item base premiums (100 for sword)
- Item-specific modifiers: cursed adds 50% of that item's base premium
- Policy-wide: first insurance adds 10% of policy base premium
- Fee at the end

For a cursed sword: 100 (base) + 50 (curse: 50% of 100) + 10 (first insurance: 10% of 100) + 5 (fee) = 165.

Note: the curse surcharge is separate from the base premium — it doesn't change the basePremium variable that the first insurance modifier applies to. The first insurance is 10% of the policy BASE premium (sum of item base premiums without modifiers).

So the implementation needs to track both:
- policyBasePremium (for policy-wide modifiers)
- totalWithItemModifiers (for item-specific surcharges added on top)

Simplest approach: accumulate item-specific surcharges separately, then:
result = policyBasePremium + itemSurcharges + policyWideMods + fee

Keep it minimal.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text:

- Did I implement features for future tests? No. I only added the cursed surcharge, which is exactly what this test demands.
- Did I add logic not demanded by the current test? No. The `if (item.cursed)` check and `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` constant are the minimal additions needed.
- Did I optimize prematurely? No. The implementation is straightforward.
- Did I refactor existing code? Only minimally: I extracted `itemBase` as a local variable to avoid computing `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` twice, and introduced `itemSurcharges` to track surcharges separately from `basePremium`. Both are necessary for correctness, not refactoring.

---

Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added a `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` constant (0.5) and an `itemSurcharges` accumulator. When iterating over non-component items, if the item is cursed, 50% of that item's base premium is added to `itemSurcharges`. The final total includes `basePremium + itemSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE`, keeping item-specific surcharges separate from the policy base premium used for policy-wide modifiers.

**Result**: All 11 tests pass (19 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal because it only adds the cursed surcharge logic that the failing test demands. The key design decision -- keeping `itemSurcharges` separate from `basePremium` -- is required by the spec's semantics (first insurance applies to base premium, not to item surcharges) and is validated by the expected value of 165 = 100 + 50 + 10 + 5.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

content.text:
### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass -- `itemSurcharge` helper (new):**

```
const itemSurcharge = (item: Item, basePremium: number): number =>
  item.cursed ? basePremium * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0;
```

- Bindings: 2 (item, basePremium) = 2
- Invocations: 1 (*) = 2
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary on item.cursed) = 4
- Constants: 2 (CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE, 0) = 2

Subtotal: 10

**Refactored `quote` function:**

The `if (item.cursed)` conditional and the inline `itemBase * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` invocation inside the loop body have been replaced by a single `itemSurcharge(item, itemBase)` invocation.

Changes in the `quote` function body:
- Removed: 1 conditional (`if (item.cursed)`) = -4
- Removed: 1 invocation (`itemBase * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE`) = -2
- Removed: 1 assignment (`itemSurcharges += ...` inside the `if`) -- actually this assignment remains as `totalItemSurcharges += itemSurcharge(...)`, so no net change in assignments.
- Added: 1 invocation (`itemSurcharge(item, itemBase)`) = +2
- Net change in quote body: -4 (one fewer conditional)

**Mass Change Summary:**
- Before: ~136 (rough estimate)
- `quote` function lost 4 mass (removed conditional) but gained nothing net in invocations
- `itemSurcharge` helper added 10 mass
- After: ~142 (rough estimate)

Mass increased slightly because we extracted a helper function, adding bindings and structure. However, this is justified by Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) which takes priority over mass.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Keep naming: `quote` clearly describes generating an insurance quote. All other names are appropriate.
- Extracted `itemSurcharge` helper function (Rule 2 -- Reveals Intent):
  - The per-item surcharge calculation is now a named, self-documenting function
  - Removed a conditional from the main loop body, making the loop cleaner
  - The `itemSurcharge` function clearly communicates that surcharges are calculated per-item based on item properties
  - Renamed `itemSurcharges` to `totalItemSurcharges` to clarify it's an accumulator
  - This extraction will naturally accommodate additional surcharge types (high-enchantment) without cluttering the main loop, though that's a side benefit, not the motivation

**Trade-off:**
- Mass increased slightly (new function adds bindings/constants), but Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) explicitly trumps APP mass. The code is now more readable and each surcharge concern is isolated.

- Tests: All 11 passing.

### Step 7: Report

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `itemSurcharge` helper function; renamed `itemSurcharges` to `totalItemSurcharges` for clarity
- **Mass Change**: Slight increase (helper adds structure), justified by improved intent revelation (Rule 2 > APP)
- **Tests**: All 11 passing

Proceeding to the next test.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass -- `itemSurcharge` helper (new):**

```
const itemSurcharge = (item: Item, basePremium: number): number =>
  item.cursed ? basePremium * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0;
```

- Bindings: 2 (item, basePremium) = 2
- Invocations: 1 (*) = 2
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary on item.cursed) = 4
- Constants: 2 (CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE, 0) = 2

Subtotal: 10

**Refactored `quote` function:**

The `if (item.cursed)` conditional and the inline `itemBase * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` invocation inside the loop body have been replaced by a single `itemSurcharge(item, itemBase)` invocation.

Changes in the `quote` function body:
- Removed: 1 conditional (`if (item.cursed)`) = -4
- Removed: 1 invocation (`itemBase * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE`) = -2
- Removed: 1 assignment (`itemSurcharges += ...` inside the `if`) -- actually this assignment remains as `totalItemSurcharges += itemSurcharge(...)`, so no net change in assignments.
- Added: 1 invocation (`itemSurcharge(item, itemBase)`) = +2
- Net change in quote body: -4 (one fewer conditional)

**Mass Change Summary:**
- Before: ~136 (rough estimate)
- `quote` function lost 4 mass (removed conditional) but gained nothing net in invocations
- `itemSurcharge` helper added 10 mass
- After: ~142 (rough estimate)

Mass increased slightly because we extracted a helper function, adding bindings and structure. However, this is justified by Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) which takes priority over mass.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Keep naming: `quote` clearly describes generating an insurance quote. All other names are appropriate.
- Extracted `itemSurcharge` helper function (Rule 2 -- Reveals Intent):
  - The per-item surcharge calculation is now a named, self-documenting function
  - Removed a conditional from the main loop body, making the loop cleaner
  - The `itemSurcharge` function clearly communicates that surcharges are calculated per-item based on item properties
  - Renamed `itemSurcharges` to `totalItemSurcharges` to clarify it's an accumulator
  - This extraction will naturally accommodate additional surcharge types (high-enchantment) without cluttering the main loop, though that's a side benefit, not the motivation

**Trade-off:**
- Mass increased slightly (new function adds bindings/constants), but Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) explicitly trumps APP mass. The code is now more readable and each surcharge concern is isolated.

- Tests: All 11 passing.

content.text:
## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The change affected only the `quote` function body. The previous single return line:

```
return Math.ceil(basePremium + totalItemSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge - loyaltyDiscount(customer, basePremium) - followUpDiscount(customer, basePremium) + PROCESSING_FEE);
```

Has been replaced with:

```
const loyalty = loyaltyDiscount(customer, basePremium);
const followUp = followUpDiscount(customer, basePremium);

const totalPremium =
  basePremium +
  totalItemSurcharges +
  firstInsuranceSurcharge -
  loyalty -
  followUp;

return Math.ceil(totalPremium + PROCESSING_FEE);
```

**Before** (return line):
- Invocations: Math.ceil, +, +, +, -, loyaltyDiscount, -, followUpDiscount, + = 18
- Bindings referenced inline: basePremium, totalItemSurcharges, firstInsuranceSurcharge, customer (x2), basePremium (x2), PROCESSING_FEE = 8
- Subtotal for this section: ~26

**After** (extracted variables + return):
- New bindings: loyalty, followUp, totalPremium = 3
- Assignments: 3 new assignments = 18
- Invocations: loyaltyDiscount, followUpDiscount, +, +, +, -, -, Math.ceil, + = 18
- Bindings referenced: customer (x2), basePremium (x4), totalItemSurcharges, firstInsuranceSurcharge, loyalty, followUp, totalPremium, PROCESSING_FEE = 12
- Subtotal for this section: ~51

**Mass Change**: Mass increased slightly (~25 units) due to the additional bindings and assignments. However, this is justified because Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) takes priority over APP mass. The refactored code clearly separates:
1. Computing policy-wide modifiers (discounts/surcharges)
2. Assembling the total premium from its components
3. Adding the processing fee and rounding

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All names already clearly describe their purpose. No renaming needed.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `loyalty`, `followUp`, and `totalPremium` as named intermediate variables to break up a long, hard-to-read return expression into clearly labeled steps.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): `loyaltyDiscount` and `followUpDiscount` share similar structure but represent distinct domain concepts. Merging would reduce clarity.
- Mass: Increased slightly due to additional bindings/assignments, but this is acceptable because Rule 2 trumps APP mass.

Benefits:
- The return expression was a single line with 6 arithmetic terms and 2 function calls -- now each modifier is clearly named on its own line
- The `totalPremium` variable makes it explicit that the processing fee is separate from the premium calculation
- Consistent pattern: all policy-wide modifiers are now pre-computed as named variables before assembly
- Easier to debug: each modifier can be inspected individually

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted named variables for policy-wide modifiers (`loyalty`, `followUp`, `totalPremium`) to improve readability of the return expression (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).
- **Mass Change**: Slight increase (~25 units), justified by improved clarity (Rule 2 trumps APP).
- **Tests**: All 17 passing.

Premiums should be rounded up (Math.ceil) in MHPCO's favor. This is already implemented (line 86 of the implementation uses Math.ceil).

The test needs a case that produces a fractional result. For example, the test for 2 runes + 1 moonstone already tested 87.5 → 88. 

We need a case where the premium computation yields a non-integer. Example from spec: "a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G".

The rounding is already implemented via Math.ceil. The test should already pass if the test case is designed correctly.

A good test case: a staff (80) with enchantment 5 (not cursed), newcomer:
- 80 base + 24 (30% enchantment) + 8 (10% first insurance) + 5 = 117 — that's an integer.

Let me think of something that yields a .5:
- An amulet (60) with a loyalty discount: 60 - 12 + 6 + 5 = 59 — integer.
- 3 runes (60 block) with loyalty: 60 - 12 + 6 + 5 = 59 — integer.
- Hmm, most combinations with these base premiums and percentage rates yield integers or half-integers.

Actually, 2 runes + 1 moonstone already showed 87.5 → 88 and that test passes. The rounding is already working.

For this test, we could use a potion (40) with follow-up discount:
40 - 6 (15% of 40) + 4 (10%) + 5 = 43. But 15% of 40 = 6, that's exact.

A potion with loyalty discount: 40 - 8 (20%) + 4 (10%) + 5 = 41. Integer.

Maybe use the spec example: 197.5 G. But getting exactly 197.5 is tricky with the fixed base premiums and rates.

Let me try: a staff (80) cursed, with loyalty, newcomer quoteCount 0:
80 + 40 (50% curse) + 8 (10% first ins) - 16 (20% loyalty) + 5 = 117. Integer.

For a non-integer, we need something like 7 runes: 175 base. 175 + 17.5 + 5 = 197.5 → 198 G. 

7 runes: 7 × 25 = 175 base (no blocks since 7 is not exactly 3). With first insurance 10% = 17.5. Total = 175 + 17.5 + 5 = 197.5 → ceil = 198.

content.text:
All 23 tests still pass.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass** for the `claim` function:

```typescript
export function claim(items: Item[], incident: Incident) {
  const insuranceSum = items.reduce(
    (sum, item) => sum + INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type],
    0,
  );
  const cap = 2 * insuranceSum;

  const totalPayout = incident.damages.reduce(
    (sum, damage) => sum + (damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE),
    0,
  );

  return { payout: totalPayout, remainingCap: cap - totalPayout };
}
```

Component Count:
- Constants: 2 (0, 0, 2) = 3 (added one more 0 for the second reduce initial value)
- Bindings: 8 (items, incident, insuranceSum, sum, item, cap, totalPayout, sum, damage) = 9 (added sum and damage for the new reduce, but sum is reused name -- still counts as a binding in its scope)
- Invocations: 7 (reduce x2, sum+INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type] access, sum+..., 2*insuranceSum, damage.amount-DEDUCTIBLE, cap-totalPayout) = 14
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 2 (reduce x2) = 10
- Assignments: 0 = 0 (no more `let` or `+=` mutations -- all `const`)

Total Mass: 3 + 9 + 14 + 0 + 10 + 0 = 36

Mass Change: 44 -> 36 (delta -8)

The mass reduction comes primarily from eliminating the two assignment operations (the `let totalPayout = 0` initialization and the `totalPayout +=` mutation), each costing 6, totaling 12. This was offset by the additional binding and constant introduced by the `reduce` call, but the net result is still a significant reduction.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Keep naming: `claim` is consistent with `quote` naming convention in this codebase
- Replaced mutable `for` loop with immutable `reduce` (Rule 2: consistency with existing `insuranceSum` pattern)
- Eliminated `let` and `+=` mutation (Rule 4: fewer elements, specifically eliminating costly assignments)
- Changed `totalPayout` from `let` to `const` (immutability improvement)

Benefits:
- Consistent iteration style: both summation operations now use `reduce`
- No mutable state: `totalPayout` is now `const` instead of `let` with mutation
- Lower APP mass: 44 -> 36 (delta -8), primarily from removing assignments (mass 6 each)
- Code is more functional and declarative in style

### Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Replaced mutable `for` loop with `reduce` for payout calculation, achieving consistency with `insuranceSum` pattern and eliminating mutable state
- **Mass Change**: 44 -> 36 (delta -8)
- **Tests**: All 23 passing

**Files modified**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-19_00-48-02_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts` (lines 109-122: refactored `claim` function to use `reduce` instead of mutable `for` loop)

Proceeding to the next test.

content.text:
## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Before refactoring** (inline logic in reduce callback):
The `claim` function's reduce callback contained the full damage calculation logic inline -- matching item lookup, enchantment threshold check, damage adjustment, and deductible subtraction all in one dense block.

**After refactoring** (extracted `reimbursableDamage` helper):
The reduce callback now delegates to `reimbursableDamage`, a named function that encapsulates the business rule of "how much damage is reimbursable given an item's enchantment level."

The total mass is approximately the same (the number of operations hasn't changed), but the structural clarity has improved because:
- The `reimbursableDamage` function gives a name to the concept of calculating adjusted damage
- The `claim` function body is now easier to scan at a glance -- it clearly shows: compute insurance sum, compute cap, sum up reimbursable damages, return result
- The business rule for enchantment-based damage reduction is isolated and testable independently

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Keep naming: `claim` and `quote` are appropriate domain terms
- Extracted `reimbursableDamage` helper (Rule 2: Reveals Intent): The inline ternary that mixed null-checking, enchantment threshold comparison, and damage adjustment has been extracted into a named helper function. The name `reimbursableDamage` clearly describes what it computes -- the amount of damage eligible for reimbursement after enchantment-based adjustments and deductible.
- Mass change: Approximately neutral. The total number of operations is unchanged; we added one binding (`adjustedAmount`) and one invocation (`reimbursableDamage(...)`) while removing equivalent inline complexity from the reduce callback.
- Rule 2 improvement: The `claim` function body is now more scannable. Each step (insurance sum, cap, total payout, return) reads clearly without needing to parse nested conditional logic.

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
**Refactoring**: Extracted `reimbursableDamage` helper function from inline reduce callback logic to improve clarity (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).
**Mass Change**: Approximately neutral -- structural clarity improved without adding complexity.
**Tests**: All 25 passing.

Proceeding to the next test.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass**:

**Constants** (Mass: 1 each):
1-6: `100, 60, 80, 40, 25, 25` (BASE_PREMIUMS values), 7. `5` (PROCESSING_FEE), 8. `0.1`, 9. `0.5`, 10. `0.3`, 11. `5` (HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD), 12. `0.2`, 13. `2` (LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD), 14. `0.15`, 15. `3` (COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE), 16. `60` (COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM), 17. `"rune"`, 18. `"moonstone"`, 19. `0` (in `|| 0`), 20. `1` (in `+ 1`), 21. `0` (in items.length === 0), 22-27: `1000, 600, 800, 400, 250, 250` (INSURANCE_VALUES), 28. `100` (DEDUCTIBLE), 29. `2` (CAP_MULTIPLIER), 30. `8` (CLAIM_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD), 31. `0.5` (HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE), 32. `0` (in `?? 0`), 33. `0` (reduce init insuranceSum), 34. `0` (reduce init totalPayout), 35. `0` (default previousPayouts), 36. `0` (reduce init componentBasePremium), 37. `0` (in `{basePremium: 0, surcharges: 0}` first), 38. `0` (in `{basePremium: 0, surcharges: 0}` second)
Constants: 38 x 1 = 38

**Bindings** (Mass: 1 each):
1. `item` (isComponent param), 2. `type` (componentPremium param), 3. `count` (componentPremium param), 4. `item` (itemSurcharge param), 5. `basePremium` (itemSurcharge param), 6. `customer` (loyaltyDiscount param), 7. `basePremium` (loyaltyDiscount param), 8. `customer` (followUpDiscount param), 9. `basePremium` (followUpDiscount param), 10. `components` (componentBasePremium param), 11. `counts`, 12. `item` (for-of var), 13. `sum` (reduce cb), 14. `type` (destructured), 15. `count` (destructured), 16. `items` (nonComponentTotals param), 17. `acc` (reduce cb), 18. `item` (reduce cb), 19. `base`, 20. `customer` (quote param), 21. `items` (quote param), 22. `components` (local), 23. `nonComponents` (local), 24. `nonComponentBase` (destructured), 25. `surcharges` (destructured), 26. `basePremium` (local), 27. `totalPremium`, 28. `item` (isHighEnchantment param), 29. `item` (reimbursableDamage param), 30. `rawAmount`, 31. `adjustedAmount`, 32. `items` (claim param), 33. `incident`, 34. `previousPayouts`, 35. `insuranceSum`, 36. `cap`, 37. `sum` (reduce cb), 38. `item` (reduce cb), 39. `sum` (reduce cb), 40. `damage` (reduce cb), 41. `matchingItem`, 42. `totalPayout`, 43. `effectiveCap`, 44. `cappedPayout`, 45. `item` (filter cb in nonComponents)
Bindings: 45 x 1 = 45

**Invocations** (Mass: 2 each):
1. `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` (isComponent), 2. `componentPremium` (in componentBasePremium), 3. `Object.entries`, 4. `.reduce` (in componentBasePremium), 5. `itemSurcharge` (in nonComponentTotals), 6. `.reduce` (in nonComponentTotals), 7. `items.filter` (components), 8. `items.filter` (nonComponents), 9. `isComponent` (in filter), 10. `isComponent` (in negated filter), 11. `nonComponentTotals`, 12. `componentBasePremium`, 13. `loyaltyDiscount`, 14. `followUpDiscount`, 15. `Math.ceil`, 16. `items.reduce` (insuranceSum), 17. `incident.damages.reduce`, 18. `items.find`, 19. `reimbursableDamage`, 20. `Math.min`, 21. `Math.floor`, 22. `isHighEnchantment`
Invocations: 22 x 2 = 44

**Conditionals** (Mass: 4 each):
1. `items.length === 0`, 2. `count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` (ternary), 3. `item.cursed` (ternary), 4. `item.enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (ternary), 5. `customer.yearsAsCustomer >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (ternary), 6. `customer.quoteCount >= 1` (ternary), 7. `isHighEnchantment(item)` (ternary), 8. `item?.enchantment ?? 0` (nullish coalescing)
Conditionals: 8 x 4 = 32

**Loops** (Mass: 5 each):
1. `for (const item of components)` (componentBasePremium), 2. `.reduce` (componentBasePremium - Object.entries), 3. `.reduce` (nonComponentTotals), 4. `items.filter` (components), 5. `items.filter` (nonComponents), 6. `items.reduce` (insuranceSum), 7. `incident.damages.reduce` (totalPayout)
Loops: 7 x 5 = 35

**Assignments** (Mass: 6 each):
1. `counts = {}`, 2. `counts[item.type] = ...`
Assignments: 2 x 6 = 12

**Total Mass: 38 + 45 + 44 + 32 + 35 + 12 = 206**

Mass went up from 198 to 206. This is expected because the refactoring traded imperative assignment-heavy code (7 assignments at mass 6 = 42) for functional iteration/invocation code (more loops and invocations). The mass increased by 8.

However, per the Simple Design Rules, **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass**. The refactoring improved the code in the following ways:

1. **Separated concerns**: The `quote` function no longer mixes component-counting and non-component accumulation in a single loop. Each concern is now handled by a dedicated function.
2. **Eliminated mutable state from `quote`**: Removed 5 mutable assignments (`let basePremium`, `let totalItemSurcharges`, and their mutations). The `quote` function now reads as a pure pipeline.
3. **Named the concepts**: `isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, and `nonComponentTotals` are named functions that clearly describe what they do.
4. **Simplified `quote` body**: The main function is now 12 lines (down from 22), making the high-level flow immediately visible: filter items, compute totals, apply modifiers, return.
5. **Inlined intermediate variables**: `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `loyalty`, and `followUp` were eliminated by inlining them directly where used, since each was used exactly once and the expressions are simple enough to read inline.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Added `isComponent` predicate function, `componentBasePremium`, and `nonComponentTotals` helper functions. All existing names were already clear. **Improved.**
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Decomposed the monolithic `quote` loop into a clear pipeline: filter by category, compute each category's totals, then apply policy-wide modifiers. The `quote` function body now reads as a high-level summary. **Improved.**
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): The component-counting logic (`componentCounts`) and the non-component accumulation were interleaved before; now each has its own dedicated function. No duplication existed before or after. **Neutral.**
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Added 3 new helper functions, which increases element count but each serves a clear purpose. Per Rule 2's higher priority, this is justified. **Acceptable tradeoff.**
- Mass: 198 -> 206 (increased by 8). The increase is due to replacing assignments (mass 6) with loops/invocations (mass 5, 2). Per Rule 2 > APP, clarity improvement justifies the slight mass increase.

Benefits:
- The `quote` function body is now a readable pipeline of clear steps
- Mutable state in `quote` eliminated (5 fewer assignments)
- Each helper function has a single, named responsibility
- Easier to understand and modify each piece independently

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:

**Refactoring**: Extracted `isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, and `nonComponentTotals` helper functions. Restructured `quote` from an imperative loop with mixed concerns into a clean functional pipeline. Eliminated mutable state from `quote` function body. Inlined single-use intermediate variables (`firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `loyalty`, `followUp`).

**Mass Change**: 198 -> 206 (Rule 2 clarity improvement justified the +8 increase)

**Tests**: All 30 passing.

1. `item` (isComponent param), 2. `type` (componentPremium param), 3. `count` (componentPremium param), 4. `item` (itemSurcharge param), 5. `basePremium` (itemSurcharge param), 6. `customer` (loyaltyDiscount param), 7. `basePremium` (loyaltyDiscount param), 8. `customer` (followUpDiscount param), 9. `basePremium` (followUpDiscount param), 10. `components` (componentBasePremium param), 11. `counts`, 12. `item` (for-of var), 13. `sum` (reduce cb), 14. `type` (destructured), 15. `count` (destructured), 16. `items` (nonComponentTotals param), 17. `acc` (reduce cb), 18. `item` (reduce cb), 19. `base`, 20. `customer` (quote param), 21. `items` (quote param), 22. `components` (local), 23. `nonComponents` (local), 24. `nonComponentBase` (destructured), 25. `surcharges` (destructured), 26. `basePremium` (local), 27. `totalPremium`, 28. `item` (isHighEnchantment param), 29. `item` (reimbursableDamage param), 30. `rawAmount`, 31. `adjustedAmount`, 32. `items` (claim param), 33. `incident`, 34. `previousPayouts`, 35. `insuranceSum`, 36. `cap`, 37. `sum` (reduce cb), 38. `item` (reduce cb), 39. `sum` (reduce cb), 40. `damage` (reduce cb), 41. `matchingItem`, 42. `totalPayout`, 43. `effectiveCap`, 44. `cappedPayout`, 45. `item` (filter cb in nonComponents)
Bindings: 45 x 1 = 45

**Invocations** (Mass: 2 each):
1. `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` (isComponent), 2. `componentPremium` (in componentBasePremium), 3. `Object.entries`, 4. `.reduce` (in componentBasePremium), 5. `itemSurcharge` (in nonComponentTotals), 6. `.reduce` (in nonComponentTotals), 7. `items.filter` (components), 8. `items.filter` (nonComponents), 9. `isComponent` (in filter), 10. `isComponent` (in negated filter), 11. `nonComponentTotals`, 12. `componentBasePremium`, 13. `loyaltyDiscount`, 14. `followUpDiscount`, 15. `Math.ceil`, 16. `items.reduce` (insuranceSum), 17. `incident.damages.reduce`, 18. `items.find`, 19. `reimbursableDamage`, 20. `Math.min`, 21. `Math.floor`, 22. `isHighEnchantment`
Invocations: 22 x 2 = 44

**Conditionals** (Mass: 4 each):
1. `items.length === 0`, 2. `count === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` (ternary), 3. `item.cursed` (ternary), 4. `item.enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (ternary), 5. `customer.yearsAsCustomer >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (ternary), 6. `customer.quoteCount >= 1` (ternary), 7. `isHighEnchantment(item)` (ternary), 8. `item?.enchantment ?? 0` (nullish coalescing)
Conditionals: 8 x 4 = 32

**Loops** (Mass: 5 each):
1. `for (const item of components)` (componentBasePremium), 2. `.reduce` (componentBasePremium - Object.entries), 3. `.reduce` (nonComponentTotals), 4. `items.filter` (components), 5. `items.filter` (nonComponents), 6. `items.reduce` (insuranceSum), 7. `incident.damages.reduce` (totalPayout)
Loops: 7 x 5 = 35

**Assignments** (Mass: 6 each):
1. `counts = {}`, 2. `counts[item.type] = ...`
Assignments: 2 x 6 = 12

**Total Mass: 38 + 45 + 44 + 32 + 35 + 12 = 206**

Mass went up from 198 to 206. This is expected because the refactoring traded imperative assignment-heavy code (7 assignments at mass 6 = 42) for functional iteration/invocation code (more loops and invocations). The mass increased by 8.

However, per the Simple Design Rules, **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass**. The refactoring improved the code in the following ways:

1. **Separated concerns**: The `quote` function no longer mixes component-counting and non-component accumulation in a single loop. Each concern is now handled by a dedicated function.
2. **Eliminated mutable state from `quote`**: Removed 5 mutable assignments (`let basePremium`, `let totalItemSurcharges`, and their mutations). The `quote` function now reads as a pure pipeline.
3. **Named the concepts**: `isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, and `nonComponentTotals` are named functions that clearly describe what they do.
4. **Simplified `quote` body**: The main function is now 12 lines (down from 22), making the high-level flow immediately visible: filter items, compute totals, apply modifiers, return.
5. **Inlined intermediate variables**: `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `loyalty`, and `followUp` were eliminated by inlining them directly where used, since each was used exactly once and the expressions are simple enough to read inline.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Added `isComponent` predicate function, `componentBasePremium`, and `nonComponentTotals` helper functions. All existing names were already clear. **Improved.**
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Decomposed the monolithic `quote` loop into a clear pipeline: filter by category, compute each category's totals, then apply policy-wide modifiers. The `quote` function body now reads as a high-level summary. **Improved.**
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): The component-counting logic (`componentCounts`) and the non-component accumulation were interleaved before; now each has its own dedicated function. No duplication existed before or after. **Neutral.**
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Added 3 new helper functions, which increases element count but each serves a clear purpose. Per Rule 2's higher priority, this is justified. **Acceptable tradeoff.**
- Mass: 198 -> 206 (increased by 8). The increase is due to replacing assignments (mass 6) with loops/invocations (mass 5, 2). Per Rule 2 > APP, clarity improvement justifies the slight mass increase.

Benefits:
- The `quote` function body is now a readable pipeline of clear steps
- Mutable state in `quote` eliminated (5 fewer assignments)
- Each helper function has a single, named responsibility
- Easier to understand and modify each piece independently

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:

**Refactoring**: Extracted `isComponent`, `componentBasePremium`, and `nonComponentTotals` helper functions. Restructured `quote` from an imperative loop with mixed concerns into a clean functional pipeline. Eliminated mutable state from `quote` function body. Inlined single-use intermediate variables (`firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `loyalty`, `followUp`).

**Mass Change**: 198 -> 206 (Rule 2 clarity improvement justified the +8 increase)

content.text:
The MHPCO Claim Office kata is complete. Here's a summary of what was built:

**30 TDD tests** (all passing) covering:
- **Quote (22 tests)**: empty list, single items (sword/amulet/staff/potion), component pricing (runes, moonstones, blocks of 3), item-specific modifiers (cursed 50%, enchantment 30%), policy-wide modifiers (loyalty 20%, first insurance 10%, follow-up 15%), multi-item policies, integration examples, and premium rounding (up)
- **Claim (8 tests)**: standard reimbursement, dragon material, high enchantment (50%), both clauses, per-item deductible, cap at 2x insurance sum, cap tracking across claims, and payout rounding (down)

**Implementation files**:
- `src/claim-office.ts` — Core business logic with `quote()` and `claim()` functions
- `src/claim-office.spec.ts` — 30 test cases
- `src/cli.ts` — CLI entry point reading JSON from stdin, writing results to stdout, with error handling for unknown item types, negative damage, items not in policy, and excess damage entries
